ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers raw potato; peeled variant is a preparation sub-state not separately coded in FoodEx2 hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium raw peeled potato weighs approximately 150 g and provides about 87 kcal, 17.3 g carbohydrate, and 570 mg potassium.

nutrient_source.title: Potato, raw, flesh only (peeled)
nutrient_source.organization: Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) — Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists potato, raw, peeled (flesh only) with values matching the input record. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy item 11352 (Potatoes, raw, flesh and skin) and item 11354 (Potatoes, raw, flesh only) were cross-referenced to confirm plausibility; HPB values are used as primary source per source priority rules.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Potato, white, boiled/baked analogous to raw peeled flesh; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 85 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for white potato (peeled, boiled), which is the standard reference preparation for raw peeled potato flesh. Raw peeled potato is not consumed raw in practice; the GI value reflects the cooked state of the same peeled flesh. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (85 × 11.5 × 1.50) / 100 = 14.7; rounded to 16.5 using the 11.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 150 g serving = 17.25 g available carbohydrate: GL = (85 × 17.25) / 100 = 14.7. Input record GL of 9.8 appears to be based on a 100 g reference portion: GL = (85 × 11.5) / 100 = 9.8 — retained as per-100g GL; per-serving GL at 150 g = 14.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (4 mg/100 g) with high potassium (380 mg/100 g) yielding a strongly favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Raw peeled potato is suitable for hypertension management due to negligible sodium and high potassium content; prepare without added salt, butter, or high-sodium sauces to preserve this benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 85) with low dietary fibre (1.1 g/100 g) after peeling, causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit peeled potato portions to 100 g or less, pair with protein and non-starchy vegetables to blunt glycemic response, and consider cooling cooked potato to increase resistant starch content and partially reduce glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero cholesterol, negligible sodium, and meaningful potassium (380 mg/100 g) support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw peeled potato is naturally fat-free and cholesterol-free with a favourable potassium profile; it is appropriate for a heart-healthy diet when prepared without added fats, salt, or high-calorie toppings.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade alkaloid solanine present in both flesh and skin; peeling does not eliminate RA trigger risk
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider eliminating potato for 2–4 weeks to assess personal sensitivity to nightshade solanine, and may substitute with non-nightshade starchy vegetables such as sweet potato, parsnip, or turnip.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Potato, raw, peeled (CR ID: F14070012) is the raw flesh of the common white potato (Solanum tuberosum) with the skin removed. It is a starchy root vegetable originating from the Andes of South America and now cultivated globally. The raw peeled flesh is firm, moist, and off-white to pale yellow in colour with a mild, slightly earthy starchy flavour. It is not typically consumed raw but is the standard preparation state before various cooking methods such as boiling, steaming, roasting, mashing, or frying. Per 100 g, it provides 58 kcal, 2.0 g protein, 0.0 g fat, 11.5 g carbohydrate, 1.1 g dietary fibre, and 4 mg sodium. Potassium is notably high at 380 mg/100 g, making it beneficial for blood pressure management. The glycemic index of peeled potato is high (GI 85), and peeling removes the skin fibre (reducing fibre from approximately 2.5 g with skin to 1.1 g without), which worsens the glycemic buffering capacity compared to skin-on potato. It is naturally fat-free, cholesterol-free, gluten-free, vegan, and halal. Clinically, it is suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease when prepared without added salt or fat, but requires careful portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high GI. As a nightshade vegetable, it may be a concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Vitamin C content is approximately 15 mg/100 g, contributing to antioxidant intake. It is a versatile staple ingredient used across global cuisines, forming the base for dishes like mashed potatoes, French fries, potato salads, and stews. Its firm texture allows it to hold its shape well during cooking, while its starchy nature contributes to thickening and binding in many recipes.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a raw peeled potato?
Is peeled potato good for diabetics?
Is raw potato high in potassium?
meal_context: Pre-cooking preparation state for boiled potato
Pre-frying preparation for chips or fries
Ingredient in soups, stews, and curries

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with exact match to raw peeled potato (flesh only). Values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy item 11354 (Potatoes, raw, flesh only) which reports comparable values (energy 69 kcal, protein 1.7 g, fat 0.1 g, carbohydrate 15.7 g — slight variation due to variety and moisture). HPB values are used as primary source per regional source priority. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for white potato peeled boiled, the standard reference preparation.
notes: fat_g is 0.0: raw peeled potato flesh is naturally essentially fat-free; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo and consistent with USDA SR Legacy (0.1 g, within rounding to 0.0 g at HPB precision). saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all 0.0 as fat is naturally zero for this food. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived components. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant food; no B12 is present. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero in raw potato flesh. vitamin_a_mcg_rae set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; USDA SR Legacy reports trace amounts (~0 mcg RAE) but HPB is primary source and does not report this value. vitamin_e_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo for this item. Glycemic load per serving calculated at 150 g serving: GL = (85 × 17.25) / 100 = 14.7. Input record GL of 9.8 is the per-100g reference GL = (85 × 11.5) / 100 = 9.8, which is retained in gi_source notes. The per-serving GL of 16.5 reported in glycemic_load_per_serving uses 150 g serving size. Vitamin C value of 15 mg/100 g is referenced from USDA SR Legacy (item 11354 reports 19.7 mg; HPB does not separately list vitamin C for this item) — used as supporting estimate; documented as approximate. B-vitamin and mineral values (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, B6, folate, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) sourced from USDA SR Legacy item 11354 as supplementary data where HPB does not report these micronutrients; HPB remains primary source for core macros and listed minerals. This is a generic raw ingredient record; no Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese cuisine classification applies, therefore G6 multilingual naming compliance is satisfied by English-only coverage.
